perspectives.challenge: A flat site fee ignores fleet-size differences inside each segment: a 60-machine enterprise site and a 20-machine one pay the same, which the larger site will exploit and the smaller will resent. The enterprise customer asked for site-level pricing, but that was in the context of not paying for idle machines — not a promise that one price fits all sites. Consider size bands within the site fee.

SentinelIQ's public £49/machine/month anchor lets its reps unbundle your site fee: at a 35-machine average enterprise site, your proposed site fee implies a per-machine figure they will compare against their list price minus volume discounts. Prepare a value defence — OEM field response and downtime results — or the comparison happens without you framing it.

The 18-month contribution constraint is binding, and onboarding cost is front-loaded at £900/machine. If adoption lands at the high end early, year-one contribution gets worse, not better — the model must show the switching value of adoption against the 18-month test, and Finance should see the phased-onboarding plan before approving.

Service capacity of one engineer per ~200 connected machines caps credible year-one adoption regardless of demand. If the subscription sells faster than hiring, alert response degrades exactly when reference customers are forming first impressions.

A quarantined document containing SentinelIQ's non-public discount floors exists inside the client. Ensure no price level in this strategy is justified by, or numerically anchored to, that material — the price architecture must be defensible from public and client-owned evidence alone, and the quarantine must be noted in the decision log.

missingTests: Willingness-to-pay validation of the proposed site-fee levels in both segments before board approval
A second mid-market customer interview to corroborate flat-fee preference beyond a single account
Sensitivity of the recommendation to mid-market adoption falling to the stale 2018 benchmark of ~8%
